The oral application for a separate issues hearing will be dismissed.

Neither party was afforded the opportunity to agitate costs of and incidental to the respondent’s oral application. An order will be made that the costs of and incidental to the respondent’s oral application be reserved.

For all of the above reasons, I make the orders as set out herein.

desc.zh: 赋予法院裁量将部分争点单独审理的权力,以促节省成本、缩窄争点或更早结案。
desc.en: Gives the court a discretion to hear some issues separately, to save cost, narrow issues or reach an earlier conclusion.

desc.zh: 在欺诈、程序不公、情势变更致困苦等情形下撤销或变更已作的 s 79 财产命令。
desc.en: Allows setting aside or varying s 79 property orders for fraud, miscarriage of justice, or hardship from changed circumstances.

desc.zh: 在未披露、不当影响、显失公平、情势重大变更致困苦等情形下撤销约束性财务协议。
desc.en: Allows a binding financial agreement to be set aside for non-disclosure, undue influence, unconscionability or material change causing hardship.

desc.zh: 就是否存在事实婚关系及其起讫作出宣告,据以行使事实婚财产管辖。
desc.en: Declares whether and when a de facto relationship existed, founding de facto property jurisdiction.

desc.zh: 界定「事实婚关系」的构成要素(共同生活的性质等多因素综合判断)。
desc.en: Defines a 'de facto relationship' through a multi-factor assessment of living together as a couple.

summary.zh: 婚姻缔结后,双方整体关系(婚前/中/后贡献)均落入 Pt VIII。
summary.en: Once married, the parties' whole relationship falls within Pt VIII.

prop.zh: 已婚则无须再单独认定事实婚以行使财产管辖。
prop.en: Marriage means there is no need to separately establish a de facto relationship for property jurisdiction.

summary.zh: 分项审理裁量的适用考量。

prop.zh: 分项须有助于实质省时省费。
prop.en: Separate issues must offer real savings of time and cost.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

CAMPTON J:

These reasons determine an oral application of Mr Houtem (“the respondent”) made on 21 April 2025 for a separate issues hearing of a list of five issues as contained in Exhibit 1, or in the alternative, a separate issues hearing of the first and second issues in Exhibit 1, pursuant to r 10.10 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”).
The oral application is opposed by Ms Houtem (“the applicant”).
For the reasons that follow, the respondent’s oral application for a separate issues hearing pursuant to r 10.10 of the Rules is dismissed.

BACKGROUND

The applicant was born in 1988. She is currently 38 years old. The respondent was born in 1982. He is currently 43 years old. They commenced cohabitation in 2010 and married in 2011. In 2011, the parties first child, X was born. They separated for the first time in May 2015.
Final consent orders were made on 13 August 2015 by the Family Court of Australia (as it then was) in relation to the parenting of their child and pursuant to s 79 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) as to the adjustment of their property (“the 2015 consent orders”).
The parties resumed their relationship in 2016. In 2017, the parties second child, Y was born. They separated for a second time, the respondent contending separation occurred in early 2019 and the applicant contends separation occurred in late 2021.
On 5 August 2022, the applicant filed an Initiating Application in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) seeking that the 2015 consent orders be set aside and other orders as to the division of the property between she and the respondent.
On 20 October 2022, a consent order was made in the following terms:
That the Final Orders be set aside pursuant to s79A of the Family Law Act 1975 (cth), as the Applicant and Respondent did not divorce and reconciled following the making of the Final Orders.

(As per the original)
On 26 September 2023, the parties entered consent orders pursuant to s 79 of the Act adjusting their (then) property between them and compromising their dispute as to the parenting of their children (“the 2023 consent orders”). Contemporaneously with the making of those orders, the parties entered a Binding Financial Agreement pursuant to s 90C of the Act as to their rights as to spousal maintenance, a Binding Child Support Agreement, and a Deed of Release pursuant to s 95 of the Succession Act 2006 (NSW), each dated 18 September 2023.
An Order for Divorce was made in 2024.
The applicant at an earlier time in this litigation contended that she and the respondent recommenced a relationship from August 2022. Her contentions as to the characteristics of that resumed relationship may have changed during the current litigation. As I understand it, she currently contends that she and the respondent lived together in a de facto relationship as defined by s 4AA of the Act from at least June 2024 until April 2025.
The respondent contends the final separation between he and the applicant occurred in early 2019, and that after that time, any relationship between them was not more than “a casual and sporadic sexual” interaction.
On 29 August 2025, the applicant filed an Initiating Application in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) seeking unparticularised orders as to property division. The Response to an Initiating Application of the respondent filed on 22 September 2025 sought that the applicant’s Initiating Application be dismissed.
In 2025, the applicant gave birth to the third child of the parties, Z. It is uncontroversial that the respondent is the father of the child.
The proceeding was transferred to the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 1) on 26 February 2026.

The relief sought by the parties

The Amended Initiating Application of the applicant filed on 23 March 2026 and as orally amended in Court on 21 April 2026 broadly seeks that:
(a) A declaration be made pursuant to s 60F of the Act that the child, Z is a child of a marriage;

(b) Specified 2023 consent orders be set aside pursuant to s 79A(1)(a) of the Act by way of a miscarriage of justice occasioned by:

(i) the respondent’s fraud by supressing evidence;

(ii) unconscionable conduct; and

(iii) undue influence;

(c) The same specified 2023 consent orders be set aside pursuant to s 79A(1)(d) of the Act, being that subsequent to the birth of the child, Z in 2025, the applicant has had the full-time care and responsibility of the three children of the parties causing her physical, emotional and financial hardship, and consequential “inequity”;

(d) The Binding Financial Agreement dated 18 September 2023 be set aside pursuant to s 90K(1)(a), (d) and/or (e) being that the agreement was obtained by way of: non‑disclosure of a material matter, undue influence, unconscionable conduct, and upon the basis that since the making of the agreement there has been a material change in circumstances as a result of the birth of the child, Z and as a result of that change, the applicant will suffer hardship if the agreement is not set aside;

or in the alternative:

(e) A declaration be made pursuant to s 90RD of the Act that the parties were in a de facto relationship that commenced on June 2024 and terminated in April 2025; and

(f) A declaration be made that the child Z is a child of that relationship;

and thereafter:
(g) An order that the respondent pay to the applicant by way of property division “a capital sum which is equivalent to 70% of the net asset pool”; and

(h) Pursuant to s 72 of the Act, or in the alternative, s 90SE of the Act, the respondent pay maintenance to the applicant of $2,500 per week for a period of five years.

The Response to an Initiating Application of the respondent as filed on 22 September 2025 and amended on 17 April 2026, seeks that the Initiating Application of the applicant filed on 5 August 2022 as amended on 24 March 2026 be dismissed.

The respondent’s application for a separate issues hearing

During the case management hearing on 21 April 2026, the respondent obtained leave to make an oral application for the listing of a separate issues hearing pursuant to r 10.10 of the Rules as to the list of issues identified in Exhibit 1.
Exhibit 1 records:
Issue 1: Whether [Z] born [in 2025] (“Child”) is a child of a marriage for the purpose of s 60F of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth).
Issue 2: Alternatively, whether:
there was a de facto relationship between the parties at the time the Child was conceived/born; and
whether the Child is a child of a de facto relationship between the parties for the purpose of s 90RD of the Family Law Act.
Issue 3: If the answer to Issue 1, is answered, “Yes”, whether:
the Consent Orders numbered 36, 37, 38 and 41 made on 26 September 2023 (“Impugned Consent Orders”) ought to be set aside pursuant to s 79A(1)(d) of the Family Law Act on the Grounds in the Applicant’s Points of Claim (“POC”), paragraphs 14 to 17.
The Binding Financial Agreement made on 18 September 2023 (“BFA”) ought to be set aside pursuant to s 90K(1)(d) of the Family Law Act on the Grounds in POC, paragraphs 14 to 17.
Issue 4: Whether the Impugned Consent Orders ought to be set aside pursuant to s 79A(1)(a) of the Family Law Act on the other Grounds in POC, paragraphs 3 to 13.
Issue 5: Whether the BFA ought to be set aside pursuant to s 90K(1)(a) or (e), on the other Grounds in POC, paragraphs 3 to 13.
(As per the original)
The applicant opposed the oral application.

THE LAW

Rule 10.10 of the Rules provides that:
(1) A party may apply for a decision on any issue, if the decision may:
(a) dispose of all or part of the proceeding; or

(b) make a trial unnecessary; or

(c) make a trial substantially shorter; or

(d) save substantial costs.
(2) An application under this rule must be made by filing an application in accordance with the approved form.
Rule 10.11 of the Rules further provides that:
(1) On an application under this Part, the court may:
(a) dismiss any part of the proceeding; or

(b) decide an issue; or

(c) make a final order on any issue; or

(d) order a hearing about an issue or fact; or

(e) with the consent of the parties, order arbitration about the proceeding or a part of the proceeding.

(2) If the court makes orders against a party who is claiming relief against the party who obtains the orders, the court may stay execution on, or other enforcement of, the orders until determination of that claim.
For the purposes of r 10.11, the word “issue” may be an issue of fact or an issue of law. The construction of the rule is broad, providing the Court with a wide discretion to utilise the powers identified in the rule with reference to the Act.
In Willmann & Willmann (No 3) [2022] FedCFamC1F 827 the relevant considerations as to the exercise of discretion to make an order pursuant to r 10.10 and r 10.11 of the Rules was identified:
In Marley & Ormonde [2020] FamCA 1046 (“Marley & Ormonde”), DCJ McClelland set out the considerations to be taken into account in determining whether to make an order for a separate determination of an issue, as summarised by Hallen AsJ (as his Honour then was) in Southwell v Bennett [2010] NSW 1372 at [15]-[17]. Those considerations include:
15. ...
(e) As a general rule, the discretionary power to order separate determination of a question should be approached with caution.

(f) In exercising its discretion, the overriding purpose of the Civil Procedure Act 2005, namely the just, quick and cheap resolution of the real issues in the proceedings (s 56) must be given effect.

(g) Generally, all questions of fact and law should be determined at the one time. If the court is to depart from that position, the party seeking the separate determination of a question must satisfy the court that it would be “just and convenient” for that order to be made.

(h) While it may appear attractive, superficially, to order the trial of a separate question, experience often shows that it will not be so, for example, because of the complications that can arise in relation to appeals, or to overlapping factual issues, or to questions of credit, if the same witnesses have to give evidence in relation to a question that is separated and those questions that are not.

...
(j) Before a question is to be separately determined, it must be possible to clearly see that it will facilitate the quicker and cheaper resolution of the proceedings.

(k) Where findings as to the credit of a witness is, or of witnesses are, or may be, involved in the consideration of the evidence relevant to the question, it is inappropriate to order a separate trial.

(l) Often, a separate question is heard on the basis of:
(i) agreed statements of fact;

(ii) a narrow point to be determined; and
(iii) a hearing that is able to be conducted within a short time (or a short time relative to the total length if the hearing of the separate question were not to be dealt with).
(m) Factors that tend to support the making of an order, include that the separate determination of the question may:
(i) contribute to the saving of time and cost by substantially narrowing the issues for trial, or even lead to disposal of the proceedings;

(ii) contribute to the settlement of the litigation.
(n) It may be appropriate to determine a separate question, even if it will not resolve all the issues, provided that there is a strong prospect that the parties will agree upon the result when the core of the dispute has been decided, or if the decision will obviate unnecessary and expensive hearings of other questions. The determination of the one question should enable a sensible reassessment of litigation risks, which is generally likely to encourage some form of settlement discussions.

(o) Relevantly, one factor that may tell against the making of an order would be where there is likely to be a significant overlap between the evidence adduced on the hearing of the separate question and at trial — possibly involving the calling of the same witnesses at both stages of the hearing of the proceeding. There is always a risk of inconsistent findings arising from determination of separate questions.

(p) Whilst the decision is ultimately one for the court to determine, it will have regard to the attitude of the parties.

(q) It is a relevant consideration to weigh the time likely to be taken in the hearing of a separate question and the availability of hearing dates for that purpose, against the time and expense of a substantive hearing and the length of time likely to elapse before such a hearing will take place.

(r) Each case will have its own dynamics that dictate the relative importance of various factors to be considered in exercising the discretion conferred.

(s) It is necessary that there be precision, both in formulating the question, and in specifying the facts upon which it is to be decided.

(Citations omitted)
The equivalent legislation in this jurisdiction to that contained in the Civil Procedure Act (NSW) is s 95 of the Act which provides:
95 Overarching purpose of the family law practice and procedure provisions

(1) The overarching purpose of the family law practice and procedure provisions is to facilitate the just resolution of disputes:
(a) in a way that ensures the safety of families and children; and

(b) in relation to proceedings under this Act in which the best interests of a child are the paramount consideration—in a way that promotes the best interests of the child; and

(c) according to law; and

(d) as quickly, inexpensively and efficiently as possible.
Note: For family law practice and procedure provisions, see subsection (4).

(2) Without limiting subsection (1), the overarching purpose includes the following objectives in relation to proceedings under this Act:
(a) the just determination of all such proceedings;

(b) the efficient use of the judicial and administrative resources available for the purposes of courts exercising jurisdiction in such proceedings;

(c) the efficient disposal of the overall caseload of courts exercising jurisdiction in such proceedings;

(d) the disposal of all such proceedings in a timely manner;

(e) the resolution of disputes at a cost that is proportionate to the importance and complexity of the matters in dispute.
(3) The family law practice and procedure provisions must be interpreted and applied, and any power conferred or duty imposed by them (including the power to make applicable Rules of Court) must be exercised or carried out, in the way that best promotes the overarching purpose.

(4) The family law practice and procedure provisions are the following, so far as they apply in relation to proceedings under this Act:
(a) the applicable Rules of Court;

(b) any other provision made by or under this Act, or any other Act, with respect to the practice and procedure of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia or any other court.
(Emphasis in original)
Rule 1.04 of the Rules provides:
1.04 Overarching purpose

(1) The overarching purpose of these Rules, as provided by section 95 of the Family Law Act, is to facilitate the just resolution of disputes according to law and as quickly, inexpensively and efficiently as possible.

...

(2) Parties to family law proceedings must conduct the proceeding (including negotiations for settlement of the dispute to which the proceeding relates) in a way that is consistent with the overarching purpose.

...

(3) A party’s lawyer must, in the conduct of a proceeding before the court (including negotiations for settlement) on the party’s behalf:
(a) take account of the duty imposed on the party referred to in subrule (2); and

(b) assist the party to comply with the duty.
The starting position is that all questions of fact and law should be heard and finally determined together, unless there is a good reason to order that the separate determination of a particular issue would give effect to the overarching purpose of the Rules and the Act.
The Court’s task in determining whether such warrant exists in this matter requires balancing considerations as to whether the separate listing will achieve a just determination in the circumstances of the case, efficiently using the resources of the Court so as to dispose of the proceedings in a timely manner at a financial and resource-based cost that is proportionate to the importance and complexity of the matters in dispute.

CONSIDERATION

The respondent submits that a separate issues hearing directed to the five matters identified in Exhibit 1 would promote the interests of justice because it, on the acceptance of his case, would determine the whole of the proceedings as to property and maintenance.
In the alternative, he submits that a determination of Issue 1 and Issue 2 in Exhibit 1 would substantially narrow and affect the conduct of the proceeding going forward, limiting the extent to which the respondent could rely on grounds pleaded pursuant to s 79A and s 90K of the Act as quickly, inexpensively and efficiently as possible.
It is helpful to unpack the context of the respondent’s oral application for a separate issues hearing.
If I understand the respondent’s contentions correctly, he asserts that as a matter of statutory construction, the child Z is “not a child of the [or a] marriage” for the purposes of s 79A(1)(d) and s 90K(1)(d) of the Act. If established, he contends that the relief prosecuted pursuant to those sources of power is bound to fail and ought to be summarily dismissed.
He submits that, as the child Z was conceived and born after the Order for Divorce came into effect, she is a child born after the termination of the marriage. He appears to implicitly identify a different concept for the purposes of the Act of “ex-nuptial” children. He asserts this characterisation is supported by the respondent’s contentions that the child was conceived at a time that the parties were in a de facto relationship, and that even on the applicant’s case, a child born of a de facto relationship does not come within the province of s 79A(1)(d) and s 90K(1)(d) of the Act.
The respondent develops this submission to the effect that the absence of a reasonable prospect of success of the applicant’s claims pursuant to these sources of power will produce costs savings and promote the earlier resolution of the proceedings, including avoiding the necessity for the obtaining of significant ch 7 single expert evidence as to valuation.
He further contends that the issues to be the subject of a separate issues hearing as identified in Exhibit 1, at least as to the first and second subject matters, are narrow and that any evidence relevant to these issues would be confined.
The applicant submits that as a matter of practice and procedure, all questions of fact and law should be determined in a single trial event.
It is her submission that a determination of her claims pursuant to s 79A of the Act are multifaceted by way of different grounds, as is her relief to set aside the s 90C Binding Financial Agreement, both going to subject matters of non-disclosure, undue influence and unconscionability, in addition to whether the child, Z is a child of a marriage pursuant to the Act or a child of a de facto relationship. Each in turn are relevant to determinations as to hardship.
It is challenging to distil the extent of the five issues in Exhibit 1 sought to be separately heard in a single hearing. As to the s 79A(1)(a) claim identified in Issue 4 of Exhibit 1, the Full Court has identified that as a starting point, s 79A proceedings should not be divided into two separate hearings, the first being limited to whether the order should be set aside. The considered course is for one hearing considering whether there has been a miscarriage of justice in the relevant sense and if so, whether the order should be varied or set aside and whether any order should be made in lieu of the original order. The interpretation of what is sought in Issue 4 of Exhibit 1 appears to be akin to a hearing as to whether a miscarriage of justice is established by reason of the specified grounds in the applicant’s points of claim.
No submissions were made as to whether the applicant’s claim pursuant to s 79A(1)(a) and s 90K(1)(a) or (e) may, so as to identify a miscarriage of justice and/or for the purposes of establishing hardship, require retrospective ch 7 single valuation evidence of the property of the parties at the date of the orders and entry into the Binding Financial Agreement on 18 September 2023. Similar evidence is likely to be required as to the value of property interests at the time of the trial, at least for the purposes of consideration of the third stage of the s 79 process (if achieved), as to the exercise of discretion to vary or set aside the earlier order. This evidence would also be relevant to establishing hardship.
Although not subject to submission, further considerations as to Anshun estoppel may arise dependent on the subject matter determined in the first hearing event.
The underscore of the respondent’s contentions as to why the separate hearing of all five issues in Exhibit 1 achieved the objectives of the Act and Rules had, at its core, an absence of reasonable prospects of success in the prosecution of the applicant’s claims upon the basis that his case is accepted. He has not made an application for summary dismissal pursuant to s 102QAB(2)(b) of the Act. If such application was made, it is a matter for him to establish that the applicant has “no reasonable prospect of successfully prosecuting the proceedings or that part of the proceedings”. The determination of a claim for summary dismissal does not resolve disputed facts but takes the applicant’s case at its highest (Bigg v Suzi [1998] FamCA 14; (1998) FLC 92-799).
As to the listing of Issue 1 and Issue 2 in Exhibit 1 for separate hearing, both parties referred to s 60F of the Act. That section provides as follows:
Subdivision D—Interpretation—how this Act applies to certain children

...

60F Certain children are children of marriage etc.

(1) For the purposes of this Act, a child is (subject to subsections (2), (3) and (4)) a child of a marriage if:
(a) the child is the child of both parties to the marriage, whether born before or after the marriage; or

(b) the child is adopted after the marriage by both parties to the marriage, or by either of them with the consent of the other.
(2) A reference in this Act to a child of a marriage includes a reference to a child of:
(a) a marriage that has been terminated by divorce or annulled (in Australia or elsewhere); or

(b) a marriage that has been terminated by the death of one party to the marriage.
The Full Court in Public Trustee & Gilbert [1991] FamCA 10; (1991) FLC 92-211 held that for the purposes of s 79A(1)(d) of the Act, the child whose care, welfare and development is affected by the order must be a child of the marriage as defined by the Act.
I was not taken to any source of power within the Act to make a “declaration” as to whether the child Z is “a child of a marriage”. It would seem to me, without hearing further submissions, that the parties are in dispute as to this factual finding. Submissions were not clear as to whether a competent appeal may lie by a party dissatisfied with that factual finding made at a separate issues hearing. In due course it may be necessary to consider if a finding as to the status of a child determines a substantive right.
The potential of satellite appeals, while other parts of the litigation remain unresolved, may cast dark shadows of delay and increased costs, weighing against the determination of this separate issue.
As to Issue 2 in Exhibit 1, the rights of the applicant by way of s 79A of the Act are premised upon the existence of the marriage between herself and the respondent, not any de facto relationship between them. A discrete determination of the s 90RD jurisdictional issue would not determine the remaining claims of the applicant pursuant to s 79A(1)(a) or s 90K(1)(a) or (e) of the Act. It also could be the subject of appeal by a dissatisfied party.
Again, although not the subject of direct submission as to Issue 2, the Full Court in Kowalski & Kowalski [1992] FamCA 54; (1993) FLC 92-342 held that once a marriage has been entered between the parties, the entire relationship between them, whether arising out of the contributions before, during or after the marriage was entered into or dissolved, falls within the ambit of Pt VIII of the Act. If that principle:
(a) Still has application, the separate issue hearing as to the s 90RD jurisdictional determination as to whether the parties were in a “de facto relationship” as defined by s 4AA of the Act after the Order for Divorce would be of no utility; and

(b) If it no longer has application, the jurisdictional issue as to whether the parties were in a “de facto relationship” is a factual determination to be made on the correctness standard. Neither party identified the extent of the evidence to be adduced on this contest and hence, the resources of the parties and the court to be applied to the determination.

It may be that the same witnesses would need to be called at both possible hearings, leading to a risk of possible inconsistent findings arising from the determination of the same questions. Credit appears to be a central issue in the litigation. Evidence may include but not be limited to matters going to contributions as a possible adjunct to the s 90RD determination.
There is no agreed statement of facts on the separate questions or issues identified in Exhibit 1. It is not readily apparent that a hearing of those matters could be conducted within a short period of time, or a significantly shorter period, than a determination of all facts and law in the matter at a single trial event.
The conduct of the litigation thus far does not promote a conclusion that determination of the separate issues will generate a strong prospect of success in concluding the litigation to obviate the need for further trial events. The parties currently exhibit an appetite for litigation.

CONCLUSION

The exercise of discretion to make a procedural order for a separate hearing of the five issues, or in the alternative, the first two issues in Exhibit 1 while superficially attractive, will be refused in circumstances where I am not satisfied that the respondent has established that there would be a significant saving of costs or substantial narrowing of issues, or that it would lead to an earlier disposal of the proceeding, such that the overriding purpose of the Act and the Rules would be progressed by way of such separate issues hearing. I find that it is just and convenient for all questions of fact and law to be determined in a single trial event.
The oral application for a separate issues hearing will be dismissed.
Neither party was afforded the opportunity to agitate costs of and incidental to the respondent’s oral application. An order will be made that the costs of and incidental to the respondent’s oral application be reserved.
For all of the above reasons, I make the orders as set out herein.
